data.narrative: The student completed Module 2 by diagnosing logs as a chronological trust boundary, identifying how a newline in the POST /links url field could forge an admin login entry, and selecting escaping over stripping to preserve forensic evidence safely. Their reflection showed strong understanding of the logging tradeoff: minimal logging reduces noise but can hide security evidence unless boundary violations are surfaced at an inspectable level.

dimension_scores.observation: Formed a specific explanation for how forged log entries were created and how to test the mitigation.
dimension_scores.evidence: Planned to inject a newline followed by fake log text and confirm the result stayed on one log line.

dimension_scores.observation: Read logs as a narrative, noticing line count, metadata attribution, and context integrity rather than only searching for errors.
dimension_scores.evidence: Described the core problem as discontinuity and context-blindness, where line-splitting introduces fake events and over-filtering removes connective tissue.

dimension_scores.observation: Provided a clear before-and-after malicious payload test that demonstrates the failure and the fix.
dimension_scores.evidence: Used a payload with a newline and fake admin login text, then compared vulnerable two-line output with patched single-line output.

dimension_scores.observation: Explained the fix and verification in clear operational language appropriate for incident handoff.
dimension_scores.evidence: Stated that SIEM tools and log parsers would now process the attack as data, not system execution logic.

dimension_scores.observation: Traced beyond the forged line to the systemic issue: logs are a trust boundary and user input must be sanitized before logging.
dimension_scores.evidence: Said logging evidence must never mean logging the raw exploit; security evidence must itself be safely enclosed.

dimension_scores.observation: Reflected on the decision tradeoff and proposed a concrete logging policy adjustment rather than a vague remediation.
dimension_scores.evidence: Recommended minimal application logging with verbose security boundaries, logging sanitized boundary violations at WARN or another inspectable level.

strengths: Strong log literacy: the student reasoned from log structure, line count, request context, and metadata attribution rather than treating each line as automatically trustworthy.
Strong root-cause depth: the student identified logs as a trust boundary and chose escaping over stripping to preserve forensic evidence safely.
data.recommendation: In Module 3, keep the same proof discipline but make the reproduction executable and repeatable so someone else can run it and see the same failure.

public_evidence.student_action: Escaped newline and carriage-return characters in the url field before logging and verified the forged admin login text no longer became a separate log entry.
public_evidence.technical_detail: The POST /links request logging path sanitizes user-controlled url input before writing through Python logging.
public_evidence.proof: A malicious URL containing a newline plus fake admin login text produced one safe log entry with a literal \n sequence instead of two separate log lines.
public_evidence.ability: Diagnoses and fixes log injection vulnerabilities
public_evidence.reviewer_value: Shows the student can preserve security evidence while preventing attacker-controlled log forgery.

concepts_demonstrated.evidence: Explained that security evidence must be safely enclosed so it cannot alter the log narrative.
Selected escaping CR/LF characters over stripping to prevent line splitting while preserving forensic context.
Adjusted minimal-by-default logging toward sanitized boundary-violation evidence at an inspectable level.

teaching_approaches.detail: Used a malicious URL payload and before/after log output to reason about the vulnerability and fix.
